reasoning: Applied the error taxonomy (grammar, vocabulary, pronunciation, pragmatic) cross-referenced with CEFR A1 descriptors and German-English interlanguage patterns. Severity is calibrated to A1: errors on structures expected at A1 (e.g., 3rd person -s, basic past tense) are rated moderate-to-major because they should be emerging; word order transfer errors are rated major because they impede fluency and are a persistent L1 transfer pattern. L1 transfer analysis draws on German V2 rule, modal verb frame, preposition collocations (mit dem Bus), and the bekommen/become false friend.

errors.original: I have very much work this week.
errors.corrected: I had a lot of work this week.
errors.explanation: The quantifier "very much" is used as a determiner before an uncountable noun. English requires "a lot of" or "much" (in negative/question contexts). Additionally, "have" should be "had" given the past time reference implied by "this week" in past context, though present tense is arguably acceptable for ongoing situations.
errors.l1_transfer_explanation: Partial. German "sehr viel Arbeit" maps to "very much work" but English requires "a lot of work" in affirmative statements.

errors.original: I must a new system... entwerfen?
errors.corrected: I must design a new system.
errors.explanation: Missing main verb (vocabulary gap for "design") but also demonstrates German modal verb frame where the infinitive goes to the end of the clause. Student placed the object before finding the verb.
errors.l1_transfer_explanation: German modal verb frame: "Ich muss ein neues System entwerfen" - infinitive at end. Student replicated this frame in English.

errors.explanation: Used "with" instead of "by" for transport. This is a direct transfer from German "mit dem Auto".
errors.l1_transfer_explanation: German "mit + transport" directly transferred. "Ich fahre mit dem Auto" -> "I go with the car." Classic German-English transfer error.

errors.explanation: Missing 3rd person singular -s on the present simple verb.
errors.l1_transfer_explanation: Partial. German does not add -s to 3rd person ("sie nimmt" uses a different inflection pattern, not a simple suffix), so the English -s rule has no L1 support.

errors.original: One colleague, he drive very fast to work.
errors.corrected: One colleague, he drives very fast to work.
errors.explanation: Same pattern as #4 - missing 3rd person singular -s.

errors.original: Last Monday I take the train because my car was kaputt.
errors.corrected: Last Monday I took the train because my car was broken.
errors.explanation: Failed to use past tense with past time expression "last Monday". Also used German word "kaputt" instead of English "broken".
errors.l1_transfer_explanation: Partial. German past tense formation is very different (auxiliary + participle), making English irregular past forms difficult. "kaputt" is a direct L1 insertion.

errors.explanation: German V2 (verb-second) word order transferred to English. In German, when a time adverbial fronts the sentence, the finite verb stays second and the subject moves after it: "Gestern fuhr ich." In English, subject-verb order is fixed.
errors.l1_transfer_explanation: Classic V2 transfer. German "Gestern fuhr ich zur Arbeit" has verb in position 2, pushing subject to position 3. This is the most characteristic German-English transfer error at this level.

errors.original: Last Friday I go by train.
errors.corrected: Last Friday I went by train.
errors.explanation: Same pattern as #6 - present tense used with past time expression.

errors.original: Last week I go by train every day.
errors.corrected: Last week I went by train every day.
errors.explanation: Same pattern - present tense with past time expression.

errors.original: It takes thirty minutes with the car.
errors.corrected: It takes thirty minutes by car.
errors.explanation: Repeated the with/by transfer error from #3 despite earlier correction.
errors.l1_transfer_explanation: Same "mit dem Auto" transfer as #3. Persistence after correction indicates deep-rooted L1 pattern.

errors.original: The bus go every ten minutes in Munich.
errors.corrected: The bus goes every ten minutes in Munich.
errors.explanation: Missing 3rd person singular -s again. Same pattern as #4 and #5.

errors.original: People explain me how to find the building.
errors.corrected: People explain to me how to find the building.
errors.explanation: Missing "to" in the indirect object construction with "explain". English requires "explain to someone", not "explain someone".
errors.l1_transfer_explanation: German "erklaeren + dative" does not require a preposition: "Leute erklaeren mir" -> "People explain me." The dative case handles what English does with "to".

errors.original: I become nervous when I am lost in a new city.
errors.corrected: I get nervous when I am lost in a new city.
errors.explanation: Used "become" as a translation of German "bekommen" (which means "to get/receive"). English "become" means "werden" (to change into). This is one of the most well-known German-English false friends.
errors.l1_transfer_explanation: Classic false friend. German "bekommen" = English "get/receive". English "become" = German "werden". Student confused the pair.

errors.explanation: Substituted /v/ for /w/ in W-initial words. German W is pronounced /v/, and the /w/ phoneme does not exist in German.
errors.l1_transfer_explanation: German has no /w/ phoneme. German W = /v/. This is a universal German-English transfer pattern.

error_patterns.pattern: Past tense omission with past time expressions
